data.title: U.S. Tariffs Roil Gold Futures, While Greenback Steadies
data.description:
Overview

Most of the dollar's late sell-off yesterday after the White House endorsed Stephen Miran to fill the remainder of Governor Kugler's term at the Federal Reserve has been recouped today. In mostly narrow ranges, the greenback is firmer against the G10 currencies but the Canadian dollar and sterling. The dollar is also trading with a stronger bias against emerging market currencies. China reports its CPI and PPI figures tomorrow, and next week's US July CPI is expected to have risen for the third consecutive month. In an otherwise quiet session, the gold futures market was roiled by news that US Customs will be imposing a tariff on one-kilogram and 100-ounce gold bars, which previously were exempt. There is still much confusion, and some continue to suspect a mistake was made. In the spot market, gold is firm and probing the $3400 area. A higher close today would be the sixth in the past seven sessions.

Outside of Japan and Taiwan, the large bourses in the Asia Pacific region fell today to pare this week's gains. Today's losses in India, facing a 50% tariff from the US, were sufficient to turn it lower on the week. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is holding on to a small gain near midday. If sustained, it would be the fourth gain this week, and it would recoup most of last week's 2.6% decline. US index futures enjoy a firmer bias. European benchmark 10-year yields are mostly 1-2 bp higher today. The 10-year Gilt yield is making new highs for the week, near 4.57%, a six-point increase on the week. The 10-year US Treasury yield is softer, slightly below 4.25%, up about five basis points this week. September WTI slumped to $63.20 to take out last month's low before recovering back above $64.00. Today is ostensibly the deadline that President Trump had given Russia to agree to a truce.

USD: The Dollar Index rallied from July 1 through August 1, and this week's pullback nearly met the 61.8% of the rally (~97.85). It is consolidating today and is holding below yesterday's high (~98.50). It recorded the North American session low yesterday after reports suggested Stephen Miran would be nominated to finish Governor Kugler's term at the Fed. Meanwhile, the event markets favor Waller to be Powell's successor. The US economic diary is light until next Tuesday's CPI. The headline and core rates are expected to have risen in July for the third consecutive month. At the same time, a consensus appears to be forming for a rate cut at the next FOMC meeting (September 17). The Fed funds futures are discounting about a 90% chance of a September cut compared with slightly less than 40% before the July employment report. The current effective Fed funds rate (weighted average) is 4.33%. The futures market puts it at 3.74% at the end of the year. It was near 4% before the jobs report, which was up almost 10 bp from before the FOMC meeting, which the market saw the Fed's hold at the end of July, despite two governor dissents, as hawkish.

Euro: The euro rose through the (61.8%) retracement of its losses since the July 1 high (~$1.1830) in early European activity yesterday and was turned back from almost $1.1700. It was sold back to nearly $1.1610 as European dealers were closing their books. It recovered to settle higher on the day after the Miran news. It is trading quietly today between $1.1635-$1.1680. Recall that the high set after last week's poor US jobs data was slightly below $1.16. Given that the euro rallied three cents off last Friday's low, some consolidation seems likely, especially ahead of the US CPI on Tuesday.

CNY: China's large external surplus (trade and current account) is understood to mean that the yuan is undervalued. Most calls to revalue it are missing two elements. The first is the broader context of the foreign exchange market. The IMF says it is around 15% undervalued, though some economists think it is closer to 20% below fair value. Consider that the OECD's model of purchasing power parity has the yen and euro almost 55% undervalued and the Canadian dollar 21% undervalued. In fact, the only G10 currency overvalued is the Swiss franc (~17%). The US has often sought to have other countries re-value rather than accept the stigma of a dollar devaluation. If the dollar were to return to fair value against the euro and yen, it would also likely be considerably less overvalued against the yuan. That is to suggest rather than a yuan problem per se, there is a dollar problem. The dollar's overvaluation explains more than the yuan's undervaluation. The second element missing from most discussions is the process by which the foreign exchange adjustment should be made. How are we getting from here to there? This goes largely unaddressed, whether one is talking about the euro, yen, or yuan. To re-value their respective currencies, do the advocates want central banks to sell Treasuries (or Agency bonds) and dollars? With deflation (and China's July CPI and PPI will be reported tomorrow, Saturday), are critics arguing that the PBOC should hike rates? And few of the calls for a revaluation of the yuan acknowledge that last month when the US dollar rallied, the yuan was the strongest currency in emerging markets and outperformed all the G10 currencies. More immediately, this week's range was set on Monday: ~CNH7.1765-CNH7.1960. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1382 (~CNY7.1345 yesterday, the lowest since last November). The absolute value of the change in the dollar's fix this week was almost 0.40%, the most since last November.

JPY: This week's range was set on Monday and Tuesday: ~ JPY146.60-JPY148.10. The dollar nearly covered the range yesterday (~JPY146.70-JPY147.70) and remains inside yesterday's range today. The five-day moving average crossed below the 20-day moving average yesterday for the first time in a month, which is a good proxy for the near-term trend. On the upside, the dollar must get above JPY148.25 to be notable from a technical perspective. Household spending disappointed, rising 1.3% year-over-year in June, about half of what economists projected in Bloomberg's survey and down from 4.7% in May. Yet the takeaway is really the improvement this year. Consider that outside an occasional statistical fluke, household spending, which is reported in real terms, has been falling from late 2022 through late last year. In December 2024-January 2025, the first back-to-back increase was posted in two years. With today's data point, the H1 average year-over-year increase was 1.4%. In H1 24, household spending fell by 1.8% on average, year-over-year. The current account surplus narrowed to JPY1.35 trillion from JPY3.4 trillion in May. Only a small part of this narrowing can be explained by trade. The trade surplus narrowed to JPY470 bln from JPY522 bln in May. The swaps market is pricing in 14 bp of tightening this year, down from 15 bp at the end of last week.

GBP: Sterling recorded the session high yesterday, almost $1.3450 after the Bank of England announced what appears to be a hawkish cut. It made a marginal new high today. The closeness of the vote (5-4) after an initial three-way split encouraged market participants to reduce the chances of another cut in Q4. That cut was fully discounted at the close of Wednesday, but it has been reduced by a little more than a quarter. In the statement, BOE officials seemed more attentive to the upside risks of inflation. The last push higher to session highs, was following the Miran news. Sterling settled above the 20-day moving average (~$1.3395) for the first time since July 23. The $1.3465 area marks the (50%) retracement of last month's pullback, which bottomed last Friday near $1.3140. The next retracement (61.8%) is around $1.3540.

CAD: The US dollar fray support around CAD1.3725. It houses the 20-day moving average and the (50%) retracement of the gains from the July 23 low near CAD1.3575. Nearby resistance is seen near this week's high (~CAD1.3810). The greenback is pinned near yesterday's lows. Canada's July employment data is the pre-weekend data highlight in North America. In H1 25, Canada has created almost 144k jobs (~183k in H1 24). Of these, about 56k were full-time positions (~66k in H1 24). The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for a 10k increase in overall jobs (83.1k in June). The unemployment rate may return to 7%, the post-pandemic peak reached in May. Ahead of the report, the swaps market is discounting around a 30% chance of a cut at the September 17 meeting. Barring a surprisingly poor report, expectations are unlikely to change much in response to today's jobs data.

AUD: The Australian dollar came within around 5/100 of a cent from the (61.8%) retracement of the losses from the July 24 high of the year (~$0.6625) to last Friday's low (~$0.6420). Yesterday's low was near $0.6490 was seen toward the end of the European session. It is trading in about a quarter-cent range so far today above $0.6510. The Australian dollar has held its own this week, gaining about 0.70%, the second strongest G10 performance this week behind sterling (~1.2%), even though a quarter-point rate cut is widely expected next week.

MXN: As widely expected, Banxico cut its overnight rate target 25 bp to 7.75%. The quarter-point reduction follows four half-point cuts in H1 25. The easing cycle began in March 2024, and in all, five quarter-point cuts were delivered last year. The swaps market sees the terminal rate around 7.25%. Before the central bank meeting outcome was announced, Mexico reported the sharp drop in headline CPI that was expected (3.51% vs. 4.32%). The core rate stands at 4.23% (4.24% in June). Banxico forecasts an average headline pace of 4.1% and 3.8% in the core in Q3. After reaching nearly MXN18.760 in late NY morning turnover, the dollar trended back toward the lows before and after the rate announcement. It settled near MXN18.6130. The greenback is a narrow range in this week's trough. Last week's low was almost MXN18.51. This week's low, set Wednesday, was about MXN18.5825. Meanwhile, the US dollar has been sold against the Brazilian real and approached the year's low set last month near BRL5.40. The low in H2 24 was closer to BRL5.37.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Agilent: A Lean, Focused Play In Life Sciences Tools Amid Tariff Turbulence
data.description:
I researched Agilent (A) for a possible buy, but there are some short-term risk factors that may lower the stock price. While Agilent is a well-managed life sciences tool developer with a strong global presence, I plan to wait for a better price - like where it was in April - before going in for the long haul.

Agilent is the leanest and most focused of the three major companies within the Life Sciences Tools and Diagnostics [LSTD] sector. It almost exclusively does Life Science Instruments & Diagnostics or LSID, with over 285,000 laboratories across the world using its instruments. It is also midcap at $32bn in market cap, while Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) and Danaher (DHR) are both large caps. They are also diversified into contract services (TMO) and diagnostics, etc. (DHR). So if I was looking to invest solely in medical instruments, I would look at Agilent.

Financially, Agilent is also a tremendously well-managed company, as we will soon discuss. The recent Trumpian tariff war with China - and with just about everybody else - has put some pressure on companies like Agilent, which manufactures and sells across the world. The stock reached its bottom in April, during President Trump’s Liberation Day announcement, and has slowly been picking itself up. Whether the pressure amounts to an opportunity - or presents an existential risk - is what I aim to discuss here.

An article here on Seeking Alpha from April 2025 discussed the impact of the US-China tariff war on Agilent’s global business. The article noted that China accounted for nearly a fifth of Agilent’s earnings, so the 54% effective Liberation Day tariff that President Trump imposed on China may hurt the company. Among various solutions, the author proposed moving facilities out to lower tariff countries “like India and Singapore.” The author also noted that the company management did not modify 2025 guidance after the new tariffs, effectively saying that those will not have much effect on the company’s bottom-line.

A fresh quarter has now arrived, and we are now in a better position to assess the effect of Trump’s tariffs on the company. The company declared earnings in May, and their next earnings will be in late August. There is material from a Jefferies conference from June. We will look into all of that.

Interestingly, in that Jefferies Conference, the new CEO Padraig McDonnell goes into the tariff discussion right at the outset, underscoring its importance for the company. Speaking about the Ignite Transformation campaign, the CEO tells us that “it's really important as you see with tariffs, that's a real engine that's going to deliver value over time.”

What is this Ignite program? The program began in late 2023 as a multi-year operational and digital transformation effort “to drive revenue growth and margin expansion — by increasing our execution capabilities.” It wasn’t directed at the tariff scenario, as such, but some of its aspects were surely informed by the 2018-19 tariff regime, also under President Trump, who launched it against China. These aspects, discussed below, could now help address some of the tariff distress.

Supply Chain Resilience Amid Tariffs

The program includes things like optimizing global operations and modernizing its supply chain. Agilent manufactures its laboratory products globally, and also sells them globally. One of these countries, accounting for a major fraction of its business, is China. So, if the tariff war on Chinese imports escalates, it could hurt both component sourcing in China, and pricing in China and elsewhere. Ignite aims to shift more manufacturing to lower tariff countries, and also diversify suppliers as a matter of principle. These steps could help with tariff risk.

Operational Efficiency as a Hedge Against Trade Costs

Another aspect of Ignite is digital transformation and automation. The aim is to make the company more modern, cut operational costs, and streamline operation. These may help the company absorb higher external costs like tariffs, without passing them on to customers in a cost sensitive market like China.

Geographic Sales Diversification

Ignite also aims to diversify geographic sales, so if the US-China tariff war creates tensions, it could realign towards Europe, India or Southeast Asia. However, this is easier said than done given how Trump has now started trade wars with many other countries, including India. Still, Agilent could use this program to choose between the lesser evil in terms of tax regimes.

Agilent’s Business Segments

Agilent had three primary business segments - Life Sciences & Applied Markets Group [LSAG], Diagnostics & Genomics Group [DGG] and Agilent CrossLab Group [ACG]. As of Q1 2025, Agilent merged its life sciences and diagnostics businesses into a combined LDG segment to better align with customer markets (the first reference to this merger occurs in JPM in Jan 2025).

Last year, the LSAG group, which produces and sells analytical instruments (e.g. liquid and gas chromatographs, mass spectrometers, spectroscopy systems), was Agilent’s largest segment with $3.22 billion revenue (~49% of total). However, demand has been soft recently due to weaker pharma/biotech capital spending and China market headwinds – FY2024 LSAG revenue declined 8% year-over-year (after a 4% drop in 2023, see page 6). Operating margins remain strong (~27–29%, see earlier citation), reflecting Agilent’s leading market positions in chromatography and mass spectrometry.

The erstwhile DGG segment offered reagents, instruments, and software for clinical diagnostics, genomics, and pathology. DGG made $1.65bn last year, which was ~25% of total, despite a 6% decline over softness in routine diagnostics and research funding. The segment still managed an operating margin of 19%. Future growth is expected from rising demand for cancer diagnostics, genomic testing, and Agilent’s expanded nucleic acid therapeutic manufacturing services (e.g., oligonucleotide production). In this regard, Agilent acquired the Canadian CDMO BIOVECTRA last year for around $925mn, which is also the company’s first foray into services for the therapeutics industry.

Finally, we have the ACG segment, which is focused on services, consumables, and lab software that support Agilent’s and other vendors’ instruments in the field. ACG generates highly recurring revenue, with over 85% contract renewal rates. Last year, it had $1.64bn in revenue, and there was a 5% growth in the segment. This shows that even when new instrument capital is slowing, aftermarket services are in high demand. ACG is also their most profitable segment, with a 32% operating margin.

Financial Analysis

Despite a slowing down of growth in recent years, Agilent has generally delivered solid performance over the years. It grew revenues at mid-single digit CAGR from 2018 to 2022, especially driven by the post-pandemic research spurt. They had $6.85bn in revenue in fiscal 2022, up 8% reported, 12% core. Fiscal 2023 saw roughly flat figures at $6.83 billion (core growth +1.5%). FY2024 saw a decline due to challenging market conditions, with a 4.7% drop in GAAP revenue at $6.51bn. Despite this drop, Agilent’s five-year trend is positive, up from ~$4.9B in 2016 (post-divestiture of Keysight) to $6.5B+ in 2024, reflecting organic growth and acquisitions. Management is guiding for a return to modest growth in FY2025 (projected ~$6.79–6.87B, +4.3% to 5.5% on a reported basis and 2.5% to 3.5% core) as market conditions recover.

Profitability

Like I said before, Agilent is a very well-managed company, combining healthy growth with solid profitability. Gross margins typically range in the mid-50 range, and good cost management and focus on services have improved its operating margins as well. The best segment has been CrossLab services in this respect, with a high operating margin and strong retention.

In FY2024, Agilent’s GAAP operating income was $1.54B (~22.9% operating margin), and on a non-GAAP basis (excluding certain one-time items), operating margin was 26.4%. This represents significant improvement from earlier years (e.g., ~23.5% non-GAAP op margin in 2020).

I also note that for the last 12 months, net profit margin is 17-18%, while ROE is around 18.8%. Note though that ROE has gone down from 21.4%, the 5-year average.

Agilent’s non-GAAP EPS was $5.29, which is still good despite going down 3% YoY. Interestingly, GAAP EPS was up 6% YoY, which I think I should attribute to one time adjustments. Last year, FCF produced was $1.19bn, which means Agilent converted over 80% of net income to cash. This strong FCF will help in reinvestments and share buyback programs.

Note On Q2 Earnings, And What To Look For In Q3

Agilent’s Q2 performance was strong. They reported $1.67bn in revenue, which, on a core basis, is up 5.3% YoY. Their GAAP net income was $215mn, which, possibly due to restructuring changes or elevated costs, fell 29%. However, their non-GAAP earnings rose 7% to $373mn. I find this GAAP/non-GAAP spread interesting, which suggests both short-term GAAP headwinds (a -ve) and long-term operational strength (a major +ve). As the company says, “Agilent delivers [a] strong quarter, enabled by [the] Ignite Transformation.”

Coming to individual segments, the new LDG segment had $654mn in revenue, which is up 8%. That shows the new structure has traction. Among the various segments, CrossLab had the most impressive operating margin - 32.4% - and its reported and core revenues grew 7% and 9%, respectively. Performance of the Applied Markets Group [AMG] was flat, with revenue of $301mn and no core growth. In coming quarters, investors need to figure out whether this weakness is structural, or cyclical, or merely one-off.

The company has guided between $1.645 billion and $1.675 billion in earnings for Q3. Earnings date is Aug 27. This is a 4.2% to 6.1% reported growth, with core growth of up to 3.6%. For Q3 and quarters ahead, we must look out for how well the Ignite program works in expanding margin. We need to see if AMG will rebound, and if there are currency issues following from structural changes in geopolitics and global finance. We also have to understand why there’s such a substantial spread between GAAP and non-GAAP, and whether that is driven by one time charges, or has deeper operational issues. Management commentary on tariffs, capital spending, and China demand, all need to be monitored for signals of how well Agilent can do during this difficult macro environment.

Other Aspects

Agilent’s balance sheet is strong. They have moderate debts, with cash and ST being about $1.5bn. They have been rated BBB+ and Baa1 by S&P and Moody’s, respectively. The ratings show the rating agency’s confidence in the company’s management of its leverage. Such a strong balance sheet has enabled Agilent to fund major acquisitions in recent years with low borrowing costs.

Agilent pays a modest dividend (current yield ~0.8% with a ~$0.99 per share annual dividend). Share repurchases have been a significant component of capital return despite the small dividend.

To sum up, Agilent has a strong financial profile, steady growth, expanding margins, a strong FCF, and a very solid balance sheet. These are foundations to withstand short- to midterm industry cycles, including tariff-related issues.

Growth Drivers, Short And Long Term

There are certain growth drivers in the short and long term. For the former, we must point to the CrossLab segment and the LC/MS platform as keys. Looking at the longer horizon, certain of Agilent’s strategic expansions should pay off well. These include its forays into precision medicine and high-sensitivity genomic and proteomic tools. Agilent’s 2022 acquisition of Avida Biomed, focused on next-generation sequencing [NGS] enrichment for oncology, helped it start in cancer diagnostics. Also, the BIOVECTRA acquisition moved Agilent into the CDMO space, aligning with growing pharma outsourcing and biologics manufacturing.

Risk Factors

R&D and lab spending is often cyclic, and even irregular, and Agilent is heavily exposed to that. Besides, the current tariff wars, and also the cross-country ban on sensitive intellectual properties create hurdles for Agilent’s geographic expansion. Both larger peers like Thermo Fisher Scientific and Danaher, and smaller ones like Waters, Bruker, and Shimadzu, produce a highly competitive space.

Competitive Positioning

Agilent is a leader in the LSTD sector, however it is situated between two categories of rivals - giant diversified companies like Thermo Fisher Scientific and Danaher, and focused specialists such as Waters, Bruker, and Shimadzu.

Thermo Fisher is the largest player in the industry, and directly competes with Agilent in chromatography, mass spectrometry, software, and diagnostics. Thermo Fisher’s scale lets it bundle offerings, with pricing leverages and so on; however, Agilent has core technological strength in some areas.

Danaher also competes in the LC/MS and other areas, where its portfolio includes SCIEX and Beckman Coulter. Here, too, Danaher offers bundled services and integrated workflows, whereas Agilent has technological advantages.

Other competitors include Waters Corporation (a direct competitor in LC and mass spec), Bruker (advanced MS tools), PerkinElmer (Revvity), competing in spectroscopy and diagnostics, and Shimadzu, which offers strong, cost-effective instruments in chromatography and MS—especially in Asia. Trump’s tariffs could open up competitive space for Shimadzu in Asia.

Valuation

Making a few reasonable long-term DCF assumptions, like mid-single-digit revenue growth (5–7%), moderate margin expansion, and a discount rate of ~7–8% (based on low beta and solid credit rating), I get a back of the envelope absolute DCF valuation in the range of $90s to $130s with a low FCF and high FCF growth case. In terms of relative valuation compared to peers, Agilent trades at ~20–21× forward earnings, slightly below or in line with peers like Danaher and Thermo Fisher, reflecting some discount due to recent slower growth.

To run you through my calculations, here’s the assumption chart:

Parameter

I have taken the growth rate right from what we see today, and also the margin. Agilent has a solid credit rating, so I have applied an optimistic discount rate. Now using the formula for EV, I get the following figures for the base case:

DCF Estimate:

10-year FCF CAGR: 6%

2034 FCF: ~$2.0B

Terminal Value (at year 10): ~$37B

PV of Terminal Value: ~$18.8B

PV of 10-year FCF: ~$11.5B

Enterprise Value: ~$30.3B

Net Debt (2025E): ~$2B

Equity Value: ~$28.3B

Per-share value: ~$95–100

Using a range of estimates and remembering the high sensitivity of DCF models to discount rate shifts, I get a plausible range of between $90 and $130, showing that Agilent is only slightly undervalued even in the best-case scenario.

Conclusion

Agilent is a solid life sciences instrument-focused midcap leader with strong financials, a robust portfolio, innovative technology, and some long- and short-term risk factors. I believe it will strongly survive the macro headwinds we are seeing today, which include the tariff situation and the soft spending. The Ignite attitude will help, but also Agilent’s core business aspects. The stock seems modestly undervalued, and it is a slow but steady growth stock, so I would not be averse to buying today. However, I think we are going to see some more short-term headwinds here, especially with the tariff war, so I will probably wait for a slightly better price to buy. If I do buy, I would hold Agilent for the longer term, focusing mainly on its slow and steady growth.

data.title: This 4 Fund Portfolio Gives You The Whole Shebang
data.description:
Introduction

For someone who's been reluctant to invest in ETFs in the past, the growing popularity of not only regular index funds, but covered call ones makes it easier to not only grow your income, but your overall wealth at the same time if used correctly.

In recent months, I've opened up to the idea of owning ETFs and have even thought about shifting around my whole portfolio to only consist of ETFs instead of individual stocks.

For one, managing ETFs is easier. And owning the correct ones can give you exposure to significant upside while mitigating some downside risks as well.

However, selecting the right ETFs for your portfolio all depends on your investment goals. And these four were all selected for specific reasons, giving investors the complete package, or the whole shebang.

Me personally, I'm always looking for a balance of income (slight lean towards income) with the potential to see capital appreciation to grow my wealth. In this article, I provide 4 ETFs for your consideration if you share the same investment philosophy.

Why ETFs Are Attractive

Exchange Traded Funds have been around for quite some time. But over the past 25 years ETFs have grown significantly in popularity. In the chart below, you can see how assets for ETFs have grown since 2000, and how these have a 24% CAGR in the last 5 years alone to $10.5 trillion.

And going forward, this is not expected to slow down, especially with the increasing popularity of Crypto and high income-producing ETFs like covered call funds. By 2030, total ETF assets are expected to be $20.4 trillion using a base case scenario. The bullish case scenario suggests assets will be $24 trillion.

ETFs not only provide diversification, they simplify investing for those who may not consider themselves great individual stock pickers. Or for those who work and don't have time to do extensive research.

Moreover, they can also help generate a nice stream of income while building your wealth and protecting you.

And with these four ETFs I present today, investors get the whole shebang. Without further ado, let's get into it.

#1 Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) - Income Reliability

I know Schwab's dividend ETF gets some flak for underperformance, but as previously mentioned, my goal is a balance of income and capital appreciation. When building a portfolio, value plays a vital role. And no matter what you say about SCHD, the fund does what it was designed to do, provide reliable income.

Sure, there may be better dividend-focused ETFs who have outperformed SCHD, but their low-cost structure with an expense ratio of just 0.04% means investors are only paying $4 for every $10,000 invested. And if you're planning on holding long-term, fees can eat into your returns if you select the wrong fund.

Investors also have to remember that other dividend-focused ETFs have significantly higher exposure to the Technology (XLK) sector, so they have a higher chance of outperforming.

But this is not guaranteed over the long-term. For example, two additional dividend ETFs I like are iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) and WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Fund ETF (DGRW).

Below you can see both have outperformed SCHD in total returns despite being younger. Since DGRO is the newest fund with an inception date of June 14, 2014, I use the day after to measure their total returns.

SCHD is up 198% compared to DGRO and DGRW, both of which have outperformed the S&P (SP500) over the same period. But as previously stated, both have higher exposure to tech.

iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF has 18% exposure, while WisdomTree's (WT) Dividend ETF has 23.88% exposure. The former has single-digit exposure at 9%.

Energy (XLE) and Consumer Staples (XLP) make up SCHD's top 2 sectors. And these sectors are known for stocks with solid fundamentals and yields well-above the S&P average.

Since its inception in October 2011, SCHD has performed well vs the S&P in total returns, up over 376% compared to 397%. And while past performance is no indicator of future performance, SCHD's diversified portfolio and annual rebalancing will likely provide steady returns for the foreseeable future.

#2 SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG) - Cheaper Exposure To The S&P

As the great Warren Buffett has always preached, investors should buy a S&P index fund and hold it for the long-term. SPLG gives investors this with a low expense ratio of just 0.02%.

This is lower than SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) whose expense ratio is 0.09%. Additionally, their share price is significantly cheaper at $74 at the time of writing compared to $630 for SPY.

So, owning SPLG is a no-brainer. SPY is just more popular with investors and has a much longer track record, formed in 1993 compared to 2005 for the former.

While the S&P can experience periods of underperformance, over time the market is likely to go up. Warren Buffett has also stated:

Stocks are safe for the long run and they're very unsafe for tomorrow.

The S&P is made up of over the top 500 largest and most profitable U.S. companies. And if one of those companies underperforms, it could be removed after the index rebalances. So, over time the S&P will perform well despite periods of underperformance.

Below is how the S&P has performed since 1927. During the years of 1929–1932, the index was down for four consecutive years while the early 2000's saw it down for three consecutive years. While this could happen again, over time the index will always bounce back. The last two years saw double-digit returns.

And while I don't think we will see those returns in 2025, buying the S&P for the long-term is a great way to build your wealth. And if you get a chance to buy any dips along the way, you should take the opportunity.

#3 Vanguard International High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYMI) - High Yield International Exposure

The reason for my third pick is the diversification it provides investors outside of the U.S. Although the President is investing heavily in the U.S. with AI and we could likely remain the dominant world power for the foreseeable future, getting international exposure while collecting over a 4% yield is a way to provide backup income to SCHD.

VYMI also has a low-cost structure with an expense ratio of just 0.15%. While Schwab's International Dividend Equity (SCHY) has a superior cost structure with an expense ratio of 0.08% and a cheaper share price of $27 compared to $80, VYMI's higher yield of 4.14% compared to 3.89% and broader diversification is the reason I think they're more attractive.

VYMI holds 1,556 holdings vs. 103, giving investors better diversification. I also like that the former has 21.4% exposure to emerging markets. Furthermore, according to Goldman Sachs' (GS) economists, the U.S. may no longer be the world's dominant power by 2056 with emerging economies projected to outpace America.

Although VYMI is made up of international stocks, some are likely well-known to U.S. investors with respected dividend payers like British American Tobacco (BTI), Royal Bank of Canada (RY), Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD), Unilever (UL), Manulife Financial Corporation (MFC), and Mercedez-Benz Group AG (OTCPK:MBGAF) to name a few.

Although VYMI has underperformed the S&P since its inception in February 2016, the fund performed well in comparison seeing how international companies typically fare against U.S. stocks.

But over the past year, VYMI has beaten the index in total returns, up 20.53% compared to roughly 17%. And while this is not guaranteed to continue, their dividend yield above 4%, broad diversification across more than 1,500 holdings, and projections for faster growth in emerging economies make them a viable option inside a diversified portfolio.

#4 Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) - Tech Exposure For Capital Appreciation

Last on the list is another Schwab fund, SCHG. The reason for this ETF is their high exposure to the tech sector, accounting for over 48% of its portfolio. Like their sister SCHD, they also have a low cost structure with an expense ratio of 0.04%. And what you're giving up in yield at 0.38%, you make it for in growth.

Moreover, the AI market projected to continue its strong growth over the next 5 years. North America currently is the largest market for artificial intelligence with Asia-Pacific expected to be the fastest growing market by 2030.

But as I stated if President Trump has anything to do with it, America will remain the dominant AI leader in the future. The President has signed executive orders removing any barriers to AI as this is expected to make the U.S. prosper.

And SCHG looks to benefit from the rapid expansion of AI. SCHG's portfolio is made up of some of the most valuable companies expected to benefit from the growth of AI.

NVIDIA Corp (NVDA) is its largest holding, accounting for 12.57% of their portfolio. Microsoft (MSFT), who recently saw their price soar after an impressive earnings report is the second largest holding at 10.97%.

So, while SCHG lacks in terms of yield, the ETF will likely make up for it in growth over the long-term, especially if artificial intelligence grows as expected. Since inception, the fund has significantly outperformed the S&P, up a staggering 846% since 2009 compared to 479%.

In comparison to other growth-focused ETFs like Vanguard's Mega-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (VUG) and Growth Index Fund ETF, SCHG outperformed both with MGK in second, up 819%.

Furthermore, with many of Schwab's ETFs splitting last October, their low cost structure and share price of roughly $30 make them attractive from a value standpoint. VUG's current share price is $455 and MGK's $380.45. VUG has the same expense ratio at 0.04% while MGK's, still good, is slightly higher at 0.07%.

Risks Of Underperformance

Although ETFs provide great diversification and some have delivered outperformances, their past performances are no guarantee of their future performance.

Additionally, their annual rebalances can also lead to underperformance. Furthermore, ETfFs weighted too heavily towards one sector could underperform vs the overall market, so this is something investors should be wary about when investing in index funds.

Bottom Line

If you're an investor looking for a simplistic way to generate a reliable, and steady stream of income while growing your wealth, ETFs can provide this. Additionally, selecting the right ones based on your investment goals is key.

And while any ETF can and will likely be outperformed by peers, selecting them based on an expected job should be the main goal when determining your portfolio selection.

If you're also looking for a balance of income and growth while seeing strong capital appreciation to grow your wealth, then consider this 4-fund portfolio.

data.title: 3 SWANs On Sale
data.description:
This article was coproduced with Kody Kester.

We love a good value.

If, after conducting our research and due diligence, we believe that something will add value to our life relative to what we pay, we will buy it with no hesitation.

This is akin to the following quote from Warren Buffett, which we believe can be applied to shopping as much as it can be to investing:

Price is what you pay, value is what you get.

We're generally most willing to pay for things that enhance our work productivity and improve our health and well-being. Things that we've purchased in recent years that fall into these categories include exercise equipment (e.g., a stationary bike), an electric toothbrush, an extra computer monitor, and a new laptop.

As an investor, we're also looking to buy quality companies. Think of businesses that can grow over the long haul. Companies with investment-grade balance sheets. Payouts that are well-supported and can keep growing.

Finally, we must be able to check off the value box.

The key is that we pay a valuation (i.e., fair value or below) that has a high probability of allowing me to fully participate in the growth of the business. This is because total returns are a mere function of yield plus growth, adjusted for valuation multiple changes.

With slower-growing businesses, we're looking for an adequate margin of safety (a double-digit percent discount to my fair value estimate).

For faster-growing businesses, we're not opposed to paying closer to fair value if we want to add to our position. In the latter case, the compounding potential of growth-oriented companies can often be enough to drive respectable returns.

With that in mind, we'll be highlighting three businesses that are trading at sizable discounts to our fair value estimates. Alongside a high average starting yield and some growth potential, we believe the trifecta could generate vigorous total returns over the next several years.

Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY): A Blockbuster Lineup For A Bargain

First up today is Bristol-Myers Squibb.

At a macro level, the global pharmaceutical industry has catalysts. Aging demographics and a rise in chronic diseases are tailwinds in developed countries. As is improving access to healthcare and the rise in government funding in developing countries.

For these reasons, Statista projects that the global pharmaceutical market will increase from $1.2 trillion in 2025 to top $1.5 trillion by 2030. Put into perspective, that's a 4.8% compound annual growth rate.

On a company-specific level, there's plenty to like about BMY. First, the company has nine products that have hit blockbuster levels of revenue or are on pace to do so in 2025 as of Q2 2025. That includes oncology stars like Opdivo and Revlimid, rare disease drug Reblozyl, and the blood thinner smash-hit co-owned with Pfizer (PFE) called Eliquis.

BMY's growth portfolio (including Opdivo, Yervoy, and Reblozyl) is also becoming a larger mix of its total revenue. In the second quarter, it made up $6.6 billion (~54%) of the company's total $12.3 billion in revenue - the inverse of Q2 2024. Excluding favorable foreign currency translation, these drugs posted a 17% topline growth rate. That more than offset the drop in revenue from legacy drugs.

Defensively, BMY also hopes to extend the patent on Opdivo through its Opdivo Qvantig subcutaneous injection formulation that was approved last December. Aside from this consideration, pharmaphorum also expects that it will help the drug to be more competitive. That's because the new formulation improves the ease of drug administration and decreases the time that patients must spend in clinics.

On the offensive side, BMY had 48 compounds in various phases of clinical development in its pipeline as of July 31st. These were spread out across more than 40 disease areas being studied.

In 2025, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for $6.51 in non-GAAP EPS. For 2026 and 2027, modest declines in non-GAAP EPS are expected. This is due to the upcoming patent expiration of Eliquis in 2026 and continued pressure on Revlimid.

Beyond that time, we believe BMY can generate mid-single-digit annual non-GAAP EPS growth for the foreseeable future. As newer drugs like Reblozyl, Camzyos, and Sotyktu are approved for more indications and gain market share, this should allow the company to return to growth.

BMY also has the balance sheet to be active on the M&A front. The company has an A credit rating from S&P that it can use to selectively fill gaps wherever needed in its portfolio.

BMY's 5.5% dividend yield comes in much higher than the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 1.2% yield. This is also backed up by a payout ratio that's positioned to be in the high-30% range for 2025. That should give BMY the flexibility to extend its 17-year dividend growth streak.

BMY's value seals the deal for us. From the current $45 share price (as of August 7th, 2025), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E of just 7.2 (40% weight for 2025 and 60% for 2026). This is leagues below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 17.9 and 34% less than our conservative $68 fair value estimate (11 fair value P/E ratio). If BMY meets our growth estimates and returns to fair value, it could have a 58% upside through June 2026. Over the next five years and change, BMY could be set up for 13% annual total returns.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR): Prime Real Estate At A Steal

Our next pick is Rexford Industrial Realty.

REXR focuses its attention on acquiring, managing, and redeveloping industrial assets in Southern California markets. These properties are often acquired below replacement cost in supply-constrained areas.

Recent data shows that the company owns 51 million square feet of industrial real estate in Southern California. That's spread out across 422 properties and more than 1,600 customers.

In places like Southern California, this portfolio would be impossible to replicate from scratch. This is because the supply of such land is quite limited due to zoning laws and environmental regulations. So, REXR's portfolio is certainly top-notch.

Not to mention that it's mission-critical. Approximately 30% of total U.S. port volume is handled at the Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach. That makes Southern California the fourth-biggest industrial market in the world, trailing only the rest of the United States, China, and Japan. This means there's a good chance that anything from the Asian markets is flowing through REXR's facilities.

That's reflected in the company's market rent premium. The trailing-12-month average executed lease rate for REXR's portfolio is $19.16 per square foot, which is 22% greater than the infill SoCal average of $15.65.

COO Laura Clark noted in her remarks during the Q2 2025 Earnings Call that macroeconomic and tariff uncertainty have weighed on rents in recent quarters. However, as these headwinds eventually dissipate, demand and rent growth should pick back up.

In the meantime, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for FFO per share to grow by 2.9% annually through 2027 off a 2024 base of $2.34. Beyond that time, we think that FFO per share growth can bounce back to around 6% annually.

REXR's balance sheet is solid, too. The company's net debt to EBITDA ratio is just 4x, and it has $1.8 billion of liquidity.

REXR's 4.7% dividend yield is secure as well. The REIT's payout ratio is poised to be in the low-70% range for 2025. That could provide a runway to extend its 11-year dividend growth streak.

REXR also offers some value. At the current $37 share price, the stock is priced at a forward P/FFO ratio of 15.1 (40% weight for 2025 and 60% for 2026). That's less than the 10-year average P/FFO ratio of 28 and 20% under our fair value P/FFO ratio of 19 ($46 fair value estimate). If REXR reverts to fair value and lives up to our growth estimates, it could generate a 30% return by the end of June 2026. Through 2030, annual total returns could be 12%.

Elevance Health (ELV): A Healthcare Giant With Healthy Upside

Our final pick of the day is none other than Elevance Health. Few companies are as entrenched in the U.S. healthcare system quite like ELV.

The company serves 46 million members via health plans. Its Carelon whole health solutions platform also serves around 100 million consumers.

The company is targeting 12% average annual growth in adjusted diluted EPS over the long term. We think that the company has a realistic path to achieve this feat, too.

This is because, beyond the near-term challenges, ELV has catalysts working in its favor in its Health Benefits and Carelon businesses.

As healthcare costs rise, it makes more sense for people to enroll in healthcare plans. That should lead to membership growth over time. Geographic expansion and growth in specialized populations also offer growth opportunities. Higher prescription volumes and a more favorable drug mix should also be positives for ELV.

All told, the company believes this will fuel a high-single-digit annual growth rate in revenue in the long run.

On the bottom line, ELV is working to transform its business processes through new technologies. Combined with optimizing its core businesses and remaining dedicated to underwriting discipline, the company thinks that it can bring adjusted operating margin to 6.5% to 7%. That would be a step up from the 5% figure posted in Q2 2025.

ELV's cash flow and A credit rating from S&P also gives it room to repurchase shares and complete bolt-on acquisitions to further juice adjusted diluted EPS growth.

After a FAST Graphs analyst consensus of a 9% decline in adjusted diluted EPS to $30 in 2025, the company is expected to return to growth. In 2026, a 7.2% increase in adjusted diluted EPS to $32.17 is the current consensus. For 2027, an additional 18.4% jump in adjusted diluted EPS to $38.08 is being forecasted.

ELV also sports a 2.4% dividend yield. The company's payout ratio is positioned to be in the low 20% range in 2025. That's why we think that ELV can extend its 13-year dividend growth streak.

Putting the buy case over the top, shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 9 (40% weight for 2025 and 60% for 2026). This is less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 15.2 and 25% below our $376 fair value per share estimate (12 P/E ratio). If ELV matches our growth projections and returns to fair value, it could have a 37% upside through June 2026. Annual total returns through 2030 would be 16%.

Risks To Consider

BMY, REXR, and ELV are each fundamentally solid companies with big potential upside. However, they still have risks like any other business, which could alter the investment thesis.

Key risks to BMY include the disruptive potential of patent expirations and drug pricing pressures.

The drugmaker faces key patent expirations in the coming years, including Eliquis in 2026 and Opdivo in 2028. The risk is that BMY's currently encouraging pipeline may not yield the results needed to offset these patent expirations. That could further weigh on the company's growth prospects.

Another risk to BMY in the years ahead is the potential for the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 to negotiate lower drug prices for the Medicare program. The IRA has already resulted in a significantly lower list price for Eliquis that is set to go into effect in 2026. Pomalyst is also among the ongoing drug price negotiations slated to go into effect in 2027.

The most notable risk to REXR is its geographic concentration.

The entirety of the company's property portfolio is in Southern California. This comes with risks like the potential for further strained trade relations with China. In turn, that could lead to reduced demand for warehousing if imports can't be made up elsewhere with other Asian trade partners.

Natural disaster risks like earthquakes and wildfires also figure prominently in REXR's risk profile. As I discussed in a January article, a 6.7 or stronger magnitude earthquake in California is a near guarantee over the next 30 years. If it's on the higher end of the severity spectrum, this could damage REXR's earnings capacity.

Noteworthy risks to ELV include health insurance industry scrutiny and insurance risk.

Deteriorating public opinion toward health insurers and escalating political rhetoric threaten the industry. If this hostility manifests in substantive legislative reforms, that could harm ELV's growth prospects.

Lastly, the company faces general risks as an insurer. Pandemics and the lingering effects of such an event could result in persistently higher medical costs for ELV. That could crimp its profitability.

The company must balance this risk while also keeping premiums competitive relative to peers. Otherwise, it risks losing market share to competitors.

Summary: 3 Quality Coiled Springs Worth Considering

From our perspective, BMY, REXR, and ELV are each quality companies. That's evidenced by the 13/13 Ultra SWAN quality rating for the former and the 12/13 Super SWAN ratings for the latter two in the Zen Research Terminal.

On average, the trio comes with a 4.2% dividend yield. This is more than triple the S&P 500 index's 1.2% yield. Better yet, these payouts look to be very sustainable. That's because the weighted average payout ratio percentage for the three is in the high-40% range. Data in the Zen Research Terminal illustrates that this is better than the average 65% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see for the respective industries. This should provide each the ability to build on their existing dividend growth track records.

Financially, the three are also promising. Their respective BBB+ and A credit ratings from S&P translate into a 30-year bankruptcy risk of 2.11% in the Zen Research Terminal. That equates to an effective A- average S&P credit rating.

The trifecta doesn't just offer quality, safe income, and robust balance sheets, either. Each comes with a reasonably attractive margin of safety, ranging from 20% to 34% discounts to our fair value estimates. That's why the stocks could generate an average cumulative upside of 42% by June 2026 and a 14% annual total return potential through 2030.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Trump Taps Miran For Short-Term Fed Position
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Steve Miran, head of the Council of Economic Advisers, is expected to be another dovish voice at the FOMC. (0:15) OpenAI unveils GPT 5. (2:52) Union Pacific launches freight service connecting L.A. to Chicago to compete with trucking. (4:32)

The following is an abridged transcript:

President Donald Trump nominated Stephen Miran, chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers, to serve as a Federal Reserve governor until Jan. 31, 2026.

The president had said he'd likely nominate a temporary governor to fill the seat, rather than someone who would eventually be elevated to Fed chair. Miran, if approved by the Senate, will serve the remainder of Adriana Kugler's term.

"In the meantime, we will continue to search for a permanent replacement," Trump wrote on Truth Social late Thursday. "He will do an outstanding job."

Last week, Kugler said she'd step down from the Fed board as of Aug. 8 to resume her professorship at Georgetown University.

Miran has been a critic of the Fed in the past and also popularized the “Mar-a-Lago Accord” plan for a weaker dollar (DXY).

Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says the nomination "reinforces what we were all thinking already, namely that what we now have is a much more political Fed, and a much less independent Fed, than we've seen for many, many decades. Naturally, that's going to continue to pose a structural headwind to the dollar, while in turn likely also boosting gold, as capital outflows from the States continue."

"Miran will have little-to-no credibility in the eyes of most market participants, including me, given that he will likely have pledged to vote for sizable rate cuts in order to have been nominated to fill the position," Brown said. "Hence, we know that he'll almost certainly be the most dovish 'dot' in the September SEP, and will probably be voting in favor of the largest cut he can plausibly make a case for at that meeting as well."

James Fishback, CEO at investment firm Azoria, says “I’ve known

Steve Miran for a couple of years now. He is 100% MAGA. He’s analytical (PhD in economics from Harvard) but he rolls up his sleeves. He talks to workers, small businesses, folks being affected by Jerome Powell’s awful policy of keeping interest rates near 20-year highs.”

But there is some incongruity with his past statements and his appointment by Trump, as well as the expectation that he will be voting for Trump’s preferred very-low-rates policy.

The Wall Street Journal noted that last year Miran criticized White House influence on the FOMC, saying: "Short-circuiting the revolving door between the Fed and the executive branch is critical to reducing the incentives for officials to act in the short-term political interests of the president."

And Bloomberg notes that in September he argued against cutting rates, saying that “massive easing” into GDP at 2.5%-3% with inflation also at 2.5% to 3% would mean inflation “cements” at those levels.

"The upside, if there is one, of all this, is that he's only going to be there for three meetings, limiting any 'damage' that can be done," Brown added. "That said, I think it's pretty clear that Miran's appointment sets the tone for how Trump is going to be looking at other Fed vacancies (Chair & permanent Kugler replacement), whereby loyalty to the president is the number 1 selection criterion."

OpenAI unveiled GPT‑5, which it says is its best AI system yet. The AI model has state-of-the-art performance across coding, math, writing, health, and visual perception, among other things, according to the company.

OpenAI said GPT‑5 is available to all users, with Plus subscribers getting more usage, and Pro subscribers getting access to GPT‑5 pro, a version with extended reasoning for even more comprehensive and accurate answers.

Microsoft (MSFT) CEO Satya Nadella said: "Today, GPT-5 launches across our platforms, including Microsoft 365 Copilot, Copilot, GitHub Copilot, and Azure AI Foundry. It's the most capable model yet from our partners at OpenAI, bringing powerful new advances in reasoning, coding, and chat, all trained on Azure."

GPT‑5 is a unified system with a smart, efficient model that answers most questions, a deeper reasoning model (GPT‑5 thinking) for harder problems, and a real‑time router that quickly decides which to use based on conversation type, complexity, tool needs, and explicit intent, the company added.

Elon Musk responded to Nadella on X, saying: “OpenAI is going to eat Microsoft alive.”

Meanwhile, Bloomberg reports that Tesla (TSLA) is dismantling its Dojo supercomputer team.

Peter Bannon, who led the Dojo project, is leaving the company, while remaining team members will be reassigned to other data centers and compute initiatives within Tesla. This follows the recent departure of around 20 Tesla employees who left to form a new AI startup, DensityAI.

Musk said on X, “It doesn’t make sense for Tesla to divide its resources and scale two quite different AI chip designs.” He added that all efforts will now be focused on Tesla’s upcoming AI5, AI6, and future chip architectures.

And Union Pacific (UNP) plans to launch a new, truck-competitive domestic intermodal service connecting Southern California's Inland Empire to Chicago.

The service is expected to significantly boost the rail company's intermodal capacity. The new offering is part of Union Pacific's Z train network, which is said to provide the fastest delivery of time-sensitive freight.

The company said the service will connect from the Los Angeles Basin's most active warehouse district through Union Pacific’s Inland Empire Intermodal Terminal directly to Chicago's Global 2 Intermodal Terminal, beginning on September 3.

Notably, rail customers will experience up to 20% faster intermodal service compared to current industry offerings between the key locations, with three days' transit. The service will start at five days a week with the ability to increase with growth.

Now Here’s What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

The Trade Desk (TTD) is shedding nearly a third of its value after a lackluster Q2 report and the exit of its CFO.

Intel’s (INTC) CEO reaffirms his commitment to the company amid Trump’s calls for his to resign.

And Meta (META) is reportedly tapping Blue Owl (OWL) and PIMCO for $29 billion in financing for a data center project.

Taking a look at the markets, stock index futures (SPX) (INDU) (US100:IND) are slightly higher, bolstered by tech gains. But lingering tariff concerns are limiting gains. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is easing back to around 4.25%.

Among active stocks premarket, Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) is higher after beating key metrics in its latest earnings report. Block (XYZ) is also up post-earnings as it raised guidance. Pinterest (PINS) is slumping after it missed on the bottom line.

The economic calendar is clear today.

Corporate bond spreads widened in April but ultimately ended the first half of the year close to where they began, near historically low levels. data.title: Wasatch Small Cap Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

The U.S. equity market finished with positive total returns in the second quarter, albeit with a high level of volatility. In early April, President Donald Trump unveiled a tariff plan with a far more protectionist bent than market participants had been expecting. Stocks plunged in response as investors began to factor in an environment of slower economic growth and higher inflation. This news was particularly damaging for small caps, since companies in the category tend to have above-average economic sensitivity. The picture changed significantly within a week after President Trump announced a 90-day pause on new tariffs. Investors appeared to conclude that the initial announcement was largely a negotiating ploy. The pause restored confidence and helped the market stage a quick recovery. As the quarter progressed and data related to economic growth, inflation and corporate earnings exhibited stability, stocks marched steadily higher. While major large-cap indexes achieved new highs, small cap value stocks lagged somewhat due to underperformance early in the period.

One notable element of the rally was the leadership displayed by lower-quality stocks amid investors’ effort to play catch-up with the rising market. Although we didn’t participate in this move due to our emphasis on quality, we nonetheless outperformed the Russell 2000® Value Index, which gained 4.97%. The Wasatch Small Cap Value Fund— Investor Class gained 5.72% in the second quarter. We believe the Fund’s strength relative to the benchmark in a potentially challenging period helps to illustrate the merits of our rigorous stock-selection process and our bottom-up focus on company fundamentals. Despite investors’ recent rotation into lower quality stocks, we maintain a high-quality bias in the Fund. While it’s impossible to say whether an emphasis on quality will be rewarded in any specific quarter or period, we see it as the best approach to small cap value investing over the long term.

Details of the quarter

The Fund typically has a more growth-oriented bent than the broader small cap value category. This aspect of our approach played a key role in the Fund’s second quarter results, as many of our top contributors to performance were in higher-growth sectors such as information technology, consumer discretionary and health care.

In technology, two of the primary contributors were AI-related stocks that had lagged in the first three months of the year but have since come back to life amid renewed enthusiasm about the artificial intelligence theme. Fabrinet (FN) and Nova Ltd. (NVMI) stand to benefit from the buildout of AI infrastructure. We maintained these positions through the first-quarter downturn since the companies’ fundamentals remained strong. Arlo Technologies, Inc. (ARLO), which provides do-it-yourself home security monitoring, also performed well after announcing that its subscription base had grown by more than 20%.

Universal Technical Institute, Inc. (UTI), a for-profit education provider, played a key role in the Fund’s relative strength in the consumer-discretionary sector. The company benefited from a weaker job market, accelerating fundamentals, and the potential for reduced regulation. Discount retailer Five Below, Inc. (FIVE) was another top contributor in the sector, as was Frontdoor, Inc. (FTDR), a provider of residential service plans covering homeowners for issues with appliances, HVAC systems, and other areas.

Two holdings drove the Fund’s strong performance in health care. Health Equity, Inc. (HQY) was the top contributor in the sector. The company benefited from solid financial results and signs that it’s successfully addressing a previous fraud issue. Ensign Group, Inc. (ENSG) contributed as the company’s steady characteristics have come into favor with investors.

On the other end of the spectrum, many of the Fund’s largest detractors for the quarter were companies with above-average sensitivity to macroeconomic trends. Homebuilders LGI Homes, Inc. (LGHI) and Champion Homes, Inc. (SKY) saw their stocks weaken on concerns that persistently high interest rates would crimp housing demand. Similarly, UFP Industries, Inc. (UFPI)—a wood processing company whose business is tied to homebuilding—and National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA), a storage property real estate investment trust, whose growth tends to fluctuate in line with housing turnover, were also notable laggards. Trucking company Saia, Inc. (SAIA), energy producer Magnolia Oil & Gas Corp. (MGY) and Kadant, Inc. (KAI), a supplier of machinery used in industrial processes such as paper production, were additional holdings affected by economic growth concerns. It’s important to keep in mind that these positions don’t reflect an attempt on our part to tilt the Fund’s portfolio in a cyclical direction, but rather they’re the result of our ongoing effort to achieve balance by keeping a portion of the Fund invested in stocks that could benefit if the economy accelerates.

Outlook and positioning

Striving to use stock-price volatility to add quality companies to the Fund at attractive valuations has been an important aspect of managing the Small Cap Value Fund over time. We continued to pursue this approach by using recent market fluctuations to add a position in CSW Industrials, Inc. (CSW), a provider of HVAC and plumbing components. CSW’s stock had weakened on concerns about slowing new construction, obscuring the fact that its business is primarily tied to the repair and replacement cycle. We also added Globant SA (GLOB), whose stock we think has been excessively punished by worries about competition from AI. We invested in Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR), as we believe management changes have caused investors to overlook the company’s favorable fundamental profile. Another addition of note was Freshpet, Inc. (FRPT), a company we’ve followed for a long time whose stock has recently come under pressure due to a modest slowdown in top-line growth. To maintain portfolio balance and because these holdings tend to have a cyclical tilt, we funded the purchase of their stocks by selling other economically sensitive holdings that we believed had become more fully valued.(Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Our emphasis on balance is in part a reflection of our view that the economy and markets are subject to a wide range of possible outcomes as we move into the second half of the year. On the positive side, a “soft landing” with continued low inflation, moderate growth, and accommodative U.S. Federal Reserve policy would represent an ideal result. On the other end of the spectrum, uncertainty surrounding trade and geopolitics could feed through to the economy and lead to stagflation. As bottom-up investors, we prefer to focus on company fundamentals and balanced portfolio construction rather than attempting to predict the path of the economy. This approach has held us in good stead over time, and we believe it remains particularly appropriate in the current environment.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely, Austin Bone
